findings.message: N+1 query pattern: the endpoint first fetches all item IDs and then issues a separate session.get(Item, iid) per id to read its name. On a table with N items this performs N+1 round-trips to the database, which is a clear performance regression and an unbounded-input hot path (the items table grows over time). Latency scales linearly with row count and will exhaust the connection pool / blow request-latency SLOs under modest load.
findings.suggestion: Fetch all names in a single query, e.g. `names = list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))` and return it directly. This is O(1) round-trips and removes the per-row session.get.

findings.message: Unbounded result set: `select(Item.id)` (and the resulting per-row loads) has no LIMIT/pagination. Memory and query count both grow with the size of the items table, so a large table turns this endpoint into a slow, allocation-heavy hot path that can starve the request pool.
findings.suggestion: Add pagination (limit/offset or keyset) to the query and document a maximum page size, e.g. `select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id).limit(limit).offset(offset)`.

findings.message: The function is annotated `-> list[str]` but never returns `names`; it implicitly returns `None`, which FastAPI will then try to serialize against `response_model=list[str]`. Beyond being a correctness bug, it also means the endpoint will currently 500 on every call after performing all the N+1 work — wasted DB load per request.
findings.suggestion: Add `return names` at the end of the function (and once the N+1 is fixed, return the single-query result directly).
